title: Noise spectroscopy of two-body loss as a probe of dynamical bulk viscosity in ultracold atomic gases
authors: Tingyu Zhang, Hiroyuki Tajima, Takeo Kato
abstract: We show that the correlated noise of the two-body loss current provides access to the dynamical bulk viscosity in weakly dissipative quantum gases. Starting from the Lindblad equation for weak inelastic losses, we derive the loss-current operator. After subtracting the leading Poissonian shot-noise background, the remaining noise power spectrum of two-body loss current is found proportional to the equilibrium correlation function of the contact operator. Combining this result with the exact relation between contact correlations and bulk viscosity, we demonstrate the correspondence between the measurable loss-current noise and the bulk-viscosity. Our result identifies the higher-order fluctuation of two-body loss as a probe of dynamical bulk viscosity, whose measurement has remained elusive in experiments.
summary: This paper proposes using the correlated noise generated by two-body particle loss in ultracold atomic gases to measure the dynamical bulk viscosity. By analyzing the noise power spectrum beyond the simple shot-noise background, the authors demonstrate a direct theoretical link between this measurable fluctuation and the bulk viscosity coefficient. This offers a powerful new experimental avenue for studying transport phenomena in quantum fluids.
main_problem: The research aims to establish a measurable link between the correlated noise spectrum of two-body particle loss and the dynamical bulk viscosity in weakly dissipative quantum gases.
main_result: The noise power spectrum of the two-body loss current is found to be directly proportional to the equilibrium correlation function of the contact operator, which in turn is related to the dynamical bulk viscosity via the Kubo formula.
method: The authors derive the loss-current operator from the Lindblad equation and calculate the noise power spectrum, subtracting the Poissonian background to isolate the transport-sensitive terms.
model_or_system: The system modeled is a two-component Fermi gas (ultracold atomic gas) undergoing weak inelastic two-body losses. The dynamics are governed by a Lindblad master equation, incorporating contact interactions.
key_observables: Loss-Current Noise Power Spectrum ($S_N$), Fano Factor ($F(\omega)$), and the dynamical bulk viscosity ($\zeta(\omega)$).
important_parameters: Weak-loss regime, unitarity, and the scattering length parameter ($v$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the Lindblad equation for weak losses and utilizes the Kubo formula to connect noise fluctuations to transport coefficients.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the dimensionless noise power spectrum dependence on the scattering length parameter, and Figure 2 illustrates the Fano factor vanishing at unitarity.
paper_structure: The paper proceeds by deriving the loss-current operator, calculating the noise correlator, and then establishing the proportionality between this noise spectrum and the retarded contact correlator, which is linked to the bulk viscosity via the Kubo formula.
why_interesting: This work provides a novel, measurable diagnostic tool—noise spectroscopy—to probe a fundamental, but experimentally elusive, transport property (bulk viscosity) in quantum gases.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Dimensionless noise power spectrum of the two-body loss current obtained from the second-order virial expansion with diﬀerent values of v.

FIG. 2. Fano factor for correlated noise of pure two-body noise in DC limit as a function of v. Γ is a dimensionless normalizing factor given by Eq. (38). F(0) becomes zero at the unitary limit where v = 0, due to scale invariance. Its value becomes small at large positive v, namely, the system is in BEC regime.
